title: Context Collector v1: Beads + Tasks adapters writing to World State
description:
First version of the Context Collector: reads from the deployed Beads Service and Supabase tasks table, normalises, and writes a unified world state snapshot. This is the first data actually flowing through the new architecture.

What it does:
On trigger (app open, manual refresh, or scheduled poll):
1. Call deployed Beads Service GET /api/beads/ready → normalise to world_state.beads_ready[]
2. Read Supabase tasks WHERE status != 'completed' → normalise to world_state.open_tasks[]
3. Compute derived fields: tasks_overdue, tasks_due_today, tasks_due_this_week
4. Write snapshot timestamp to world_state metadata
5. Expose via useWorldState hook in React

Note: Calendar data is NOT included in v1 (that's EPIC-COLLECT / life-5eu). The Rules Engine v1 will work with Beads + Tasks data only until Calendar is wired up.

Depends on: life-xqz (deployed Beads Service), life-57m (World State schema)
Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

title: Design and provision Supabase World State schema
description:
Create the Supabase project and schema that serves as the shared World State store for all agents. This is the central dependency for the entire intelligence layer.

Tables required:
  calendar_snapshot (id, title, start_time, end_time, duration_minutes, event_type, free_block_before_minutes, free_block_after_minutes, source, synced_at)
  tasks (id, title, category, priority, time_required_minutes, deadline, status, source, source_url, created_at, updated_at)
  rules (id, name, condition_json, threshold, cooldown_hours, enabled, created_at, updated_at)
  notification_log (id, rule_id, task_id, channel, fired_at, user_action, actioned_at)
  user_preferences (id, notifications_enabled, quiet_hours_start, quiet_hours_end, email, timezone, updated_at)
  patterns (id, observation, suggested_rule_change, confidence, status, created_at, reviewed_at)

Implementation:
1. Create Supabase project.
2. Run schema SQL in Supabase SQL editor.
3. Enable Row Level Security on all tables.
4. Add RLS policies (single-user for now: user_id = auth.uid() or fixed user ID).
5. Add VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY to .env.local and Netlify env vars.
6. Add SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY to Netlify env vars (for server-side writes).
7. Seed rules table with 4 v1 seed rules from ARD v2.

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

title: Deploy Beads Service as persistent cloud endpoint
description:
Deploy server/index.js as a persistent cloud service so cloud functions and the Context Collector can read Beads data without local machine access.

Why:
The current server/index.js runs on localhost:3001 during development. Cloud functions (Netlify, scheduled jobs) have no way to reach it. Deploying it as a persistent service eliminates the local/cloud divide for Beads data.

Implementation:
1. Choose hosting: Railway (simplest Node.js deploy), Render, or Fly.io.
2. Add Dolt sync on startup: server reads from Dolt remote (configured in life-6zf) before serving requests.
3. Add health check endpoint (already exists: GET /api/health).
4. Configure environment: BDG_DIR pointing to synced Dolt checkout, /bin/zsh available.
5. Update Vite proxy config and Netlify functions to call deployed URL in production (localhost:3001 in dev).
6. Add BEADS_SERVICE_URL to Netlify env vars.

Note: write operations (claim, close) flow through this service → Dolt remote → local bd dolt pull keeps local copy current.

Depends on: life-6zf (Dolt remote must be configured first)
Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

title: Configure Dolt remote for beads-global
description:
Set up a Dolt remote so beads-global state can be accessed from the cloud. This unblocks deploying the Beads Service.

Why this first:
The deployed Beads Service (server/index.js) needs to sync from a remote Dolt repo on startup. Without a configured remote, it can only serve whatever state was baked into the container at build time — useless for live data.

Implementation:
1. Evaluate DoltHub (managed, free tier, simple setup) vs. self-hosted Dolt server.
2. Create remote: bd dolt remote add origin <url>
3. Initial push: bd dolt push origin main
4. Verify: clone the remote on a separate machine, confirm issues are visible.
5. Add bd dolt push to the standard session-close workflow (already in CLAUDE.md).
6. Document remote URL in .notes and README.

Recommendation: DoltHub unless there are privacy concerns about issue content being in a managed service.

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

title: EPIC: Infrastructure Foundation
description:
The foundational layer that collapses the local/cloud divide and provides the shared World State store. Everything in the system depends on this epic being complete first.

Components:
1. Dolt remote configuration for beads-global (DoltHub or self-hosted)
2. Deployed Beads Service (server/index.js as a persistent cloud service)
3. Supabase World State schema (calendar_snapshot, tasks, rules, recommendations, notification_log, user_preferences, patterns tables)
4. Context Collector v1 (Beads + Tasks adapters writing to World State on event trigger)

Why this first:
Without the Dolt remote, Beads data is invisible to cloud functions. Without the deployed Beads Service, no agent can read Beads issues. Without the World State schema, no agent has anywhere to write. Without Context Collector v1, the World State is empty. Every other epic reads from or writes to this foundation.

See ARD v2 §Implementation Sequence and §Beads-as-a-Service for full rationale.

title: Build local API server bridging bd CLI to React app
description: The browser can't call bd CLI directly. A small Express server wraps bd --json commands and serves them over HTTP. Vite proxies /api/* to it during dev.
design: server/index.js: Express app with GET /api/beads/ready, /api/beads/list, /api/beads/show/:id. Each route runs bd with --json via child_process.execSync. Add proxy to vite.config.js. Add dev:full script to package.json running both servers concurrently.
close_reason: Express server in server/index.js wraps bd --json for /api/beads/ready|list|show/:id|stats. Vite proxies /api to port 3001. npm start runs both concurrently.

title: Context Reasoner: on-demand Claude recommendations from World State
description:
On-demand Claude call that takes full World State and returns rich ranked recommendations with explanations. Replaces the current rules-based scoring algorithm in LifeOrganizer.jsx.

This is the 'What should I work on right now and why?' button.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Where Claude Belongs):
Claude is given: calendar today (free blocks, committed time), open tasks (all sources, priority/deadline), Beads issues ready, recent user actions (what was accepted/dismissed). Returns: ranked list with specific reasons tied to current context, not generic priority scores.

Example output:
  1. life-b45 (Rules Engine v1) — your 2pm meeting just ended, you have 90 min free, this is a 1-2h task, and it unblocks 3 other issues
  2. Reply to Sarah's email — implicit deadline (she needs an answer today), 15 min
  3. life-57m (World State schema) — P0 blocker, nothing else can proceed without it

Implementation:
1. Create Netlify function: POST /api/recommend with worldState payload.
2. Call Anthropic API: structured prompt with world state sections, request ranked JSON response.
3. Cache response in Supabase recommendations table (invalidate on world state change).
4. Add 'Ask Claude' button to PWA recommendation panel.
5. Store user_action (accepted/dismissed) against each recommendation for Pattern Analyzer.

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — need rule firing history for context)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

title: Rules Engine v1: 4 seed rules evaluating World State
description:
Implement the deterministic Rules Engine that evaluates a seed set of rules against World State on every state change. No AI involved — pure condition evaluation.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern):
Rules are stored in Supabase rules table. Engine reads enabled rules, evaluates each condition against current World State, checks cooldown via notification_log, fires matches to Notification Dispatcher.

4 seed rules to implement:
  open-block-match: free_block.duration >= task.time_required AND task.priority IN (high, critical) AND task.status = pending → 'You have {duration} free — {task} fits'
  deadline-proximity: task.deadline - now <= 24h AND task.status != completed → 'Deadline tomorrow: {task}'
  beads-unblocked: beads_issue.all_blocked_by_closed = true AND priority <= P1 → '{issue} is now unblocked'
  stalled-deadline: days_since_progress >= 3 AND deadline - now <= 7 days → '{task} has stalled — {N} days left'

Implementation:
1. Create evaluateRules(worldState, rules) pure function.
2. Check cooldowns against notification_log.
3. Write matches to notification_log (fired_at, rule_id, context_payload).
4. Expose rule match events for Notification Dispatcher to consume.
5. Run on: app open, manual refresh, and Supabase realtime subscription to tasks/calendar_snapshot tables.

Depends on: life-4qr (Context Collector v1 — needs World State data to evaluate)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

title: EPIC: Intelligence Layer
description:
The deterministic Rules Engine and the two AI-powered reasoning agents. This is where the system earns the word 'intelligent'.

Components:
1. Rules Engine v1: evaluate 4 seed rules against World State on every state change. Deterministic, no AI, near-zero cost.
2. Context Reasoner: on-demand Claude call with full World State → ranked recommendations with explanations. User-triggered.
3. Pattern Analyzer: daily/weekly Claude call reviewing rule firing history → suggest rule adjustments. One call per cycle.

Rule evaluation is fast, cheap, and always-on. Claude is invoked at exactly two points: when the user asks for advice (Context Reasoner), and when the system reviews its own behaviour (Pattern Analyzer).

Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (Rules Engine needs World State to evaluate against)

See ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern and §Where Claude Belongs.

title: EPIC: Context Collection
description:
All structured data sources that feed the World State. Each source is a pluggable adapter in the Context Collector. This epic adds Calendar and refines the Beads/Tasks adapters from EPIC-INFRA.

Components:
- Calendar Context Collector (life-5eu revised): Google Calendar OAuth → calendar_snapshot in World State
- Corporate calendar bridge: iCal URL subscription for read-only work calendar
- Beads adapter refinement: ensure sync latency and error handling are production-grade
- Task adapter: manual tasks from Supabase tasks table → world state open_tasks

Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (World State schema must exist)

See ARD v2 §Implementation Sequence Phase 2.

title: Calendar Context Collector — Google Calendar adapter feeding World State
description:
Personal Google Calendar is the first structured source to plug into the Context Collector layer. This makes calendar data available to the Rules Engine for free-block matching and schedule-aware recommendations.

Architectural context (ARD v2):
This is a Context Collector adapter, not a standalone feature. Its output goes into the World State (Supabase calendar_snapshot), where the Rules Engine can evaluate open-block-match and schedule-aware rules against it.

Implementation plan:
1. Google Cloud Console: create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App). Scopes: calendar.readonly. Authorised origins: localhost:5173 + production URL.
2. Add VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID to .env.local and Netlify env vars.
3. Load Google Identity Services in index.html.
4. Create useGoogleCalendar hook: initTokenClient, requestAccessToken on mount, store token in sessionStorage.
5. Fetch events: GET /calendar/v3/calendars/primary/events (timeMin=now, timeMax=+7d, singleEvents=true).
6. Normalise to WorldState calendar_snapshot shape: {id, title, start, end, duration_minutes, type, free_block_before, free_block_after}.
7. Write snapshot to Supabase calendar_snapshot table (upsert on event id).
8. Handle token expiry: re-request on 401.
9. Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (World State schema must exist before this can write to it).
design: 1. Google Cloud Console: create project, enable Calendar API, create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App type). Authorized JS origins: http://localhost:5173 and https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. Authorized redirect URIs: same. Copy client ID. 2. Add VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID to .env.local (gitignored) and to Netlify env vars. 3. Load Google Identity Services in index.html: <script src='https://accounts.google.com/gsi/client'>. 4. Create useGoogleCalendar hook: tokenClient = google.accounts.oauth2.initTokenClient({client_id, scope:'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.readonly', callback}). On mount if no token, call requestAccessToken(). Store token in sessionStorage (not localStorage — expires). 5. Fetch events: GET https://www.googleapis.com/calendar/v3/calendars/primary/events?timeMin=now&timeMax=+7days&singleEvents=true&orderBy=startTime. 6. Normalise to {id, title, start, end, date, type} shape matching existing CalendarContent component. Replace MOCK_CALENDAR. 7. Handle token expiry: re-request on 401.

title: Beads write-back: claim and close from UI via local API
description:
Beads tasks are currently read-only in the UI. The accordion shows 'bd close <id>' as a text hint but cannot execute it. Since the local Express server is already running when the app is used locally, claim and close should be possible directly from the UI for that workflow.

Implementation Plan:
1. Add POST /api/beads/claim/:id to server — runs: bd update <id> --status=in_progress in ~/beads-global via zsh. Returns {ok, error}.
2. Add POST /api/beads/close/:id to server — accepts JSON body {reason}, runs: bd close <id> --reason='<reason>'. Returns {ok, error}.
3. In BeadsTaskRow expanded panel: show Claim button when task status is open/pending; Close button always visible.
4. Clicking Close reveals an inline reason input (text field + confirm/cancel) before POSTing — no modal.
5. Both buttons show tooltip 'server offline' and are disabled when /api/health check fails.
6. On success: re-fetch beads list. On failure: show inline error below the button.
7. Conservative approach: wait for server confirmation before updating UI (no optimistic update).
design: Server: add POST /api/beads/claim/:id (runs: bd update <id> --status=in_progress) and POST /api/beads/close/:id with JSON body {reason} (runs: bd close <id> --reason='<reason>'). Both run in ~/beads-global with shell zsh. Return {ok, error}. UI: in BeadsTaskRow expanded panel, show Claim button when task status is pending, Close button always. Close triggers an inline reason input (text field + confirm/cancel) before POSTing. Both buttons disabled with tooltip 'server offline' when /api/health fails. On success: refresh beads list. On failure: show inline error. Optimistic update optional — conservative approach (wait for refresh) is safer.
close_reason: Claim and close from UI fully implemented: server endpoints, hook functions, inline UI with reason input, spinner, error handling, offline fallback.

title: Connect Netlify site to GitHub for auto-deploy on push
description:
Currently deploying manually via 'netlify deploy --prod'. The Netlify site is created and linked to the repo via API but the GitHub App webhook that triggers builds on git push has not been installed. Every deploy requires a manual CLI command.

Implementation Plan:
1. Navigate to app.netlify.com/projects/life-organizer-mo → Site configuration → Build & deploy → Continuous deployment.
2. Click "Connect to GitHub" and authorize the Netlify GitHub App.
3. Select repo: mofro/life-organizer. Branch: main.
4. Set build command: npm run build. Publish directory: dist.
5. Save and trigger a test deploy by pushing a trivial commit.
6. Verify in the Netlify dashboard that the build runs and succeeds automatically.
7. Remove any manual 'netlify deploy' steps from workflow documentation.
Note: Steps 1–4 must be done in the Netlify UI — cannot be completed via CLI alone.
design: Manual step required in Netlify UI — cannot be done via CLI alone. Navigate to: app.netlify.com/projects/life-organizer-mo → Site configuration → Build & deploy → Continuous deployment → Connect to GitHub. Select mofro/life-organizer, branch: main, build command: npm run build, publish dir: dist. Verify by pushing a trivial change (e.g. comment) and watching the Netlify dashboard build log. After setup, remove manual netlify deploy from workflow docs.
close_reason: Already configured. GitHub App connected to mofro/life-organizer, deploys confirmed firing on push to main.

title: Debug and harden local API server startup
description: User experienced Beads section showing 'server offline' when running npm start. Root cause unclear — may be bd not in PATH when npm spawns child process, server crash on startup, or user ran 'npm start dev' (wrong command). Need diagnostics and hardening so failures are obvious, not silent.
design: 1. Add GET /api/health endpoint that returns {ok:true, bdPath, bdgDir, version}. 2. On server startup, call bd --version and log result — fail loudly if not found. 3. Validate ~/beads-global exists on boot; print clear error if missing. 4. Add npm run check script that validates all prereqs before starting. 5. Update README with exact command (npm start, not npm start dev) and troubleshooting section. 6. Consider wrapping concurrently output with prefixes so server vs vite logs are distinguishable.
close_reason: Implemented all 6 items from design: /api/health endpoint, startup validation (bd PATH + beads-global dir check with process.exit(1)), npm run check script, concurrently prefixes, README troubleshooting section

title: Replace mock Beads data with real bd CLI data in dashboard
description: Dashboard currently shows only manually-added tasks. Replace with real Beads issues fetched from the local API server, displayed alongside manual tasks.
design: New useBeadsTasks hook fetches /api/beads/ready on mount and refresh. Beads tasks rendered in their own section above manual tasks. Source badge shows 'beads' with link to bd show output. Include issue ID, title, priority, status, blocked_by count.
close_reason: useBeadsTasks hook fetches /api/beads/ready, normalises to task model. BeadsTaskRow component shows issue with indigo badge. Recommendations draw from both manual + Beads tasks.

title: Test artifact in Claude.ai
description: Deploy completed artifact to Claude.ai and verify all features work in that environment.
design: Test: form fields keep focus, tasks persist after refresh, recommendations appear, no console errors, mobile layout works.
close_reason: Running locally at localhost:5173 via Vite. Tested HTTP 200. Full dev environment preferable to Claude.ai artifact for local file access in future phases.

title: Implement window.storage persistence
description: Tasks must persist across browser refreshes. Claude.ai artifact environment provides window.storage API.
design: On task add/update/delete: window.storage.set('tasks', JSON.stringify(tasks)). On mount: window.storage.get('tasks').then(...). Handle missing/corrupt data gracefully.
close_reason: storageLoad/storageSave helpers try window.storage first, fall back to localStorage. Saves on every tasks change after initial load.

title: Build task dashboard UI
description: Core UI components for the Life Organizer: Dashboard, TaskList, QuickStats, RecommendationCards. Must look good and be functional.
design: Tailwind CSS styling. Components: Dashboard (layout), TaskForm (uncontrolled), TaskList (sortable), QuickStats (counts by category/priority), RecommendationCards (top 3 suggested tasks).
close_reason: Built Dashboard, QuickStats, RecommendationCards, TaskForm, TaskRow, TaskList, CalendarSection components with Tailwind.

title: Fix form input focus issues
description: Forms lose focus when using controlled inputs with object spread in onChange. Needs to work in Claude.ai artifact environment.
design: Use FormData API with uncontrolled inputs and formRef. No object spread in onChange handlers. Call formRef.current.reset() after submit.
close_reason: Used FormData API with formRef + uncontrolled inputs. form.reset() after submit. No onChange state handlers.

title: Email dispatch: Rules Engine → Resend for high-priority alerts
description:
Email delivery channel in the Notification Dispatcher. When the Rules Engine fires a high-priority rule match, send an email alert via Resend.

Architecture:
Replaces the original life-7pf (standalone Netlify cron with hardcoded deadline check). Email dispatch is now triggered by the Rules Engine, not a separate scheduled function. Any rule can be configured to notify via email channel.

Implementation:
1. Create Resend account (resend.com — free tier 3000 emails/month).
2. Add RESEND_API_KEY to Netlify env vars.
3. Create Netlify function: POST /api/notify/email with {rule_match, user_email, context}.
4. Template: subject from rule match type, body includes task title + deadline + context + direct link to PWA.
5. Deduplication: check notification_log before sending (same rule + same task + same day = skip).
6. Respect user_preferences.quiet_hours (don't send between quiet_hours_start and quiet_hours_end).
7. Channel selection: email is opted-in per rule (configurable in Settings).

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1), life-57m (World State schema — needs notification_log)
Part of: EPIC-NOTIFY (life-k6l)

title: Web Push infrastructure: background notifications via service worker
description:
True background push notifications — fires even when the PWA is not open. Requires service worker + VAPID keys + push subscription stored server-side.

Architecture:
The Rules Engine fires a rule match → Notification Dispatcher routes to push channel → Netlify function sends Web Push message → service worker receives and displays notification.

This is the background complement to the in-app notifications in life-4ov.

Implementation:
1. Generate VAPID key pair (web-push library).
2. Add VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY and VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY to Netlify env vars.
3. Service worker: handle 'push' event → display notification with title + body + action buttons.
4. PWA: on permission grant, call pushManager.subscribe() with VAPID public key. Store subscription endpoint in Supabase user_preferences.
5. Netlify function: given a rule match, call web-push.sendNotification(subscription, payload).
6. Integrate with Notification Dispatcher: push channel is selected based on user preference and notification priority.

Note: requires HTTPS in production (already satisfied by Netlify). iOS Safari support requires iOS 16.4+ with PWA installed to home screen.

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1), life-57m (World State schema — needs subscription storage)
Part of: EPIC-NOTIFY (life-k6l)

title: Conversation Intake Agent: Claude session → Beads issues and tasks
description:
Lightweight agent that extracts tasks, commitments, and decisions from Claude conversations and creates structured work items. Formalises what is already happening implicitly.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Conversation as Work Stream):
This is already happening: the ARD was generated from a Claude conversation; Beads issues were created from Claude sessions; bd remember is a primitive version. This agent makes the capture explicit and automatic.

Source → destination routing (no manual labelling):
  Work brainstorming conversation → work project Beads issues
  Personal planning conversation → Supabase life tasks
  Mixed → user disambiguates at confidence-gate

Extraction categories:
  commitments: 'I need to...', 'I should...', 'I'll...' → task
  decisions: 'We decided to...', 'Going with...' → bd remember + optional task
  open questions: 'Need to find out...', 'TBD:...' → low-priority task flagged as research
  deadlines: any time reference attached to a commitment → set deadline on task

Trigger options (start with explicit, automate later):
  A. Explicit: user clicks 'Extract tasks from this conversation'
  B. Session-end: automatic extraction on conversation close
  C. Always-on: background extraction with high confidence threshold

Implementation (start with option A):
1. Add 'Capture to Beads' / 'Capture as task' button to PWA (or Claude sidebar).
2. User pastes or the system captures recent conversation context.
3. Claude call: extract structured items (title, type, priority, deadline, source_project).
4. Confidence-gate: surface all extracted items for user review before creating.
5. User approves/edits/dismisses each item.
6. Confirmed items: bd create (for Beads) or Supabase insert (for life tasks).

Part of: EPIC-INTAKE (life-72y)

title: Corporate Bridge: iCal subscription + email forwarding setup
description:
Read-only integration with corporate (Comcast) calendar and email via pragmatic bridges that require no IT involvement.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Corporate Walled Garden):
Full Exchange/Outlook integration requires IT approval and corporate OAuth. The ceiling is iCal URL subscription + user-configured forwarding rules — ~70% of scheduling value at zero IT involvement.

Component 1: iCal URL subscription
  Outlook allows publishing a read-only iCal URL: Settings → Calendar → Shared calendars → Publish.
  Subscribe this URL in the Calendar Context Collector alongside Google Calendar.
  Provides: events, free/busy, meeting titles. Sufficient for scheduling.
  No write operations. No OAuth.

Component 2: Email forwarding rules (user-configured, documented)
  User sets up Outlook rule: selected senders or subjects → forward to personal Gmail.
  Gmail Intake Agent (life-9br) picks up forwarded emails automatically.
  Life Organizer does not configure these — just documents the setup and handles forwarded emails gracefully.

Implementation:
1. Extend Calendar Context Collector to accept multiple iCal URLs.
2. Add iCal URL input to Settings panel (store in user_preferences).
3. Poll iCal URL on same schedule as Google Calendar.
4. Write corporate events to calendar_snapshot with source='corporate'.
5. Document forwarding rule setup in README.

Depends on: life-5eu (Calendar Context Collector infrastructure)
Part of: EPIC-INTAKE (life-72y)

title: Email Intake Agent: Gmail parsing → World State tasks
description:
Claude-powered agent that reads personal Gmail, classifies each email, extracts actionable items, and writes them to World State.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Intake Layer as a First-Class Architectural Concern):
Email is where a large fraction of real commitments live — often implicit, rarely formatted as tasks. Rules can't parse 'could you take a look before Friday?' — Claude identifies the implicit ask, estimates urgency from sender context, and creates a task with appropriate confidence score.

Classification categories:
  meeting invite → create calendar event + optional prep task
  explicit deadline → create task with extracted deadline
  implicit ask → create task flagged low-confidence, surface for user confirmation
  notification/FYI → log to World State context, no task
  noise → discard

Implementation:
1. Gmail OAuth: add gmail.readonly scope to existing Google OAuth flow.
2. Poll Gmail (on schedule or app-open) for unprocessed emails (last 24h).
3. For each email: Claude call with subject + sender + body → classification JSON.
4. High-confidence results: write to Supabase tasks directly.
5. Low-confidence results: surface in PWA as 'Did you mean to add this?' prompt.
6. Track processed emails (message ID) to avoid reprocessing.
7. User can adjust classification rules in Settings.

Trigger: scheduled (daily) + manual ('check email for tasks' button in PWA)

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — output goes to World State which rules evaluate)
Part of: EPIC-INTAKE (life-72y)

title: Pattern Analyzer: daily Claude call for rule refinement
description:
Daily/weekly Netlify scheduled function that reviews rule firing history and user responses, then suggests rule adjustments. The mechanism by which the system learns from behaviour.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern):
The Pattern Analyzer has access to: current rule set, notification_log (what fired, when), user_actions (accepted/dismissed/snoozed with timestamps), patterns table (previous suggestions).

It asks Claude: 'Given this rule firing history and these user responses, what adjustments would make the rules more useful?'

Example outputs:
  'open-block-match fires at 9am but is almost never acted on before noon — suggest: add time_of_day >= 11:00 to condition'
  'stalled-deadline has 0% acceptance rate — suggest: increase threshold from 3 days to 5 days or disable'
  'I notice tasks tagged beads with priority P0 are always acted on within 1 hour of notification — suggest: create new rule for P0 beads with 30-min cooldown'

Implementation:
1. Netlify scheduled function: runs daily (or weekly initially).
2. Query notification_log + user_actions for last 7 days.
3. Claude call with rule set + history → structured suggestion JSON.
4. Write to patterns table with status='pending'.
5. Surface pending patterns in PWA Settings panel for user review.
6. User approves → update rules table. User dismisses → status='dismissed'.
7. Optional: auto-apply suggestions with confidence >= 0.9 (configurable).

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — needs firing history to analyze), life-tm6 recommended (Context Reasoner — for shared Anthropic API infrastructure)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

title: EPIC: Notification & Dispatch
description:
The Notification Dispatcher: routes Rules Engine output to the right channel (in-app, push, email) with appropriate priority and cooldown management.

Components:
1. In-app notifications (life-4ov revised): browser Notification API, Rules Engine-triggered, cooldown via notification_log
2. Web Push infrastructure: service worker registration, VAPID keys, push server (Netlify function or dedicated), background delivery
3. Email dispatch via Resend: Rules Engine → email for high-priority rule matches. Resend free tier (3000/month). Replaces standalone cron approach.

Design principle: the dispatcher is dumb routing. Intelligence (what to notify) lives in the Rules Engine. The dispatcher only decides how and whether to route (channel, deduplication, quiet hours).

Depends on: EPIC-INTEL (Rules Engine must exist to trigger dispatch)

See ARD v2 §Decision 10 and §Rules Engine Pattern.

title: EPIC: Intake Agents
description:
Agents that convert unstructured, ambiguous external signals into structured World State entries. This is the layer that captures obligations from email, corporate context, and conversations — the inputs that a simple task list would miss entirely.

Components:
1. Email Intake Agent: Gmail → classify (meeting invite / deadline / implicit ask / noise) → extract → World State. Claude-powered.
2. Corporate Bridge: iCal URL subscription for work calendar + optional email forwarding rules. Partially rule-based.
3. Conversation Intake Agent: Claude session → extract tasks/commitments/decisions → confidence-gate → Beads issues or Supabase tasks.

Key principle: source determines destination. Work brainstorming → work Beads issues. Personal planning → life tasks. No manual labelling.

Depends on: EPIC-INTEL (output flows through Rules Engine into World State)

See ARD v2 §Intake Layer as a First-Class Architectural Concern and §Conversation as Work Stream.

title: Beads close with inline reason prompt in accordion
description:
bd close requires a reason string. The close button in the accordion (once Beads write-back is built) should prompt for a reason inline before submitting — not a modal, not a default empty string.

Implementation Plan:
1. When Close is clicked: replace the button with an inline form — a single text input (placeholder: 'Reason for closing…') plus ✓ confirm and × cancel buttons.
2. Auto-focus the input when it appears.
3. Enter key confirms; Escape key cancels and restores the Close button.
4. On confirm: POST /api/beads/close/:id with {reason: inputValue.trim()}. Show a spinner on the confirm button while in-flight.
5. On success: remove task from beads list (optimistic remove) and show a brief 'Closed' toast.
6. On error: restore the Close button and show inline error text below the input.
7. Depends on: life-1kn (Beads write-back — POST /api/beads/close/:id must exist first).
design: When Close is clicked: replace the button with an inline form — a single text input (placeholder: 'Reason for closing…') and two small buttons: ✓ confirm and × cancel. On confirm: POST /api/beads/close/:id with {reason: inputValue}. Input should auto-focus on appear. Enter key confirms, Escape cancels. Show a spinner on the button while the request is in flight. On success: remove task from beads list (optimistic) and show a brief 'Closed' toast. On error: restore the Close button and show inline error text. Depends on: Beads write-back issue.
close_reason: Implemented as part of life-1kn — inline reason input with auto-focus, Enter/Escape keys, spinner, and error display is fully built in BeadsTaskRow.

title: Rules-triggered in-app notifications (Notification Dispatcher v1)
description:
First implementation of the Notification Dispatcher: when the Rules Engine fires a rule match, surface it as an in-app notification (browser Notification API) and update the recommendation display. This is the client-side half of the dispatcher — no push server required.

Architectural context (ARD v2):
The Notification Dispatcher routes Rules Engine output to push/email/in-app channels. This issue implements the in-app channel only. Web Push (background notifications) and Email dispatch are separate issues under EPIC-NOTIFY.

Implementation plan:
1. Request Notification.requestPermission() on first app load.
2. Subscribe to rule-match events from the Rules Engine (initially polled from Supabase notification_log on app open/focus).
3. For each unacknowledged rule match: fire browser Notification + update recommendation panel.
4. Track acknowledgement in Supabase notification_log (prevents re-firing within cooldown).
5. Add notification toggle in Settings panel. Store preference in Supabase user_preferences.
6. Depends on: EPIC-INTEL Rules Engine v1 (rule matches must exist to dispatch).
design: 1. On app first load, call Notification.requestPermission() — only if not already granted/denied. 2. Create checkUrgentTasks(tasks) function: finds tasks where deadline is within 24h and status !== completed. 3. For each urgent task, check localStorage key lo-notified-<id>-<YYYY-MM-DD> — if not set today, fire new Notification('Life Organizer', {body: task.title + ' is due today', icon: '/icon-192.png'}). Set the localStorage key to prevent repeat firing. 4. Call checkUrgentTasks on mount and on window focus event. 5. Add notification toggle in a minimal Settings panel (collapsible Section at bottom of app). Store preference in localStorage as lo-notifications-enabled. 6. Note: this only fires when app is open/focused. True background notifications require a push server — deferred to later.

title: Add search across manual tasks
description:
No way to find a specific task as the list grows. Need real-time text search filtering the task list, working alongside existing status filters.

Implementation Plan:
1. Add searchQuery string state to the main LifeOrganizer component.
2. Pass searchQuery and setSearchQuery down to TaskList alongside existing filter props.
3. In TaskList filter logic, apply search after status filter: tasks.filter(t => matchesStatus(t) && matchesSearch(t, searchQuery)).
4. matchesSearch(task, q): case-insensitive includes check on task.title, task.category, and task.source.
5. Add a search input to the TaskList header row — magnifying glass icon on the left, × clear button appears when the field is non-empty.
6. Debounce input by 150ms using useRef + setTimeout to avoid filtering on every keystroke.
7. No external search library needed.
design: Add searchQuery state (string) to main LifeOrganizer component. Pass to TaskList alongside filter. In TaskList filter logic, apply search after status filter: tasks.filter(t => matchesStatus(t) && matchesSearch(t, q)). matchesSearch checks t.title, t.category, t.source case-insensitively. Add search input to TaskList header row — magnifying glass icon on left, clear (×) button appears when non-empty. Debounce input 150ms via useRef setTimeout. No external library needed.
notes: Part of: EPIC-POLISH (life-u86). This is a standalone UI improvement that can proceed independently of the infrastructure and intelligence epics.

title: Mobile layout testing and polish
description:
Verify the app is usable on real mobile devices (iOS Safari, Android Chrome). The grid and flex layouts are designed to be responsive but real-device testing will surface issues that desktop DevTools miss.

Implementation Plan:
1. Test on iOS Safari: check tap target sizes on dropdowns and delete buttons (min 44×44px). Audit all inputs for font-size < 16px (triggers iOS auto-zoom — fix any offenders).
2. Test on Android Chrome: check touch scroll in task list and beads accordion. Verify no horizontal overflow at 360px width.
3. Check iOS Safari bottom bar overlap — add padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom) to main container if content is hidden.
4. Verify two-column section grid stacks to single-column at sm breakpoint. Check badge rows wrap correctly at narrow widths.
5. Audit long task titles — ensure they truncate or wrap cleanly without breaking card layout.
6. Fix any issues found. Document tested device/OS combinations in the PR description.
design: Known responsive: two-column sections stack to single-col at sm breakpoint, badge rows wrap, stats grid is 2x2 on mobile. Watch for: tap target sizes on status dropdowns and delete buttons, form field zoom-in on iOS (font-size < 16px triggers), overflowing text in task titles on narrow screens, bottom nav bar overlap on iOS Safari.
notes: Part of: EPIC-POLISH (life-u86). This is a standalone QA task that can proceed independently of the infrastructure and intelligence epics.

title: Implement basic recommendation engine
description: Algorithm that suggests which tasks to work on next based on deadlines, time available, and priority.
design: Three categories: urgent (deadline < 3 days), quick wins (timeRequired < 1h), big blocks (timeRequired >= 3h). Score and rank tasks. Return top 3 recommendations with reason.
close_reason: Scoring algorithm: deadline urgency (+100/60/30), priority boost (+40/20/5), quick wins <60m (+25), focus block fit (+20). Returns top 3.

title: Add calendar event display (mock)
description: Display upcoming calendar events alongside tasks so recommendations can account for scheduled time.
design: Static mock events array with title, start, end, type fields. Styled event cards showing today/tomorrow events. Used by recommendation engine to find free time slots.
close_reason: MOCK_CALENDAR array with today/tomorrow events. CalendarSection component displays them grouped by day.

title: EPIC: UI & Polish
description:
User-facing improvements that don't block the core system but meaningfully improve the experience. These can proceed largely independently of the infrastructure/intelligence epics.

Existing issues in this epic:
- life-86p: Search across manual tasks
- life-bvk: Mobile layout testing and polish
- life-d3h: Real PWA icons
- life-24a: Calendar view modes (day, work-week, month) — blocked by life-5eu

New work:
- Recommendation UX improvements (accept/dismiss/snooze with clear feedback)
- Settings panel (notification preferences, quiet hours, rule visibility)
- Onboarding flow (first-run experience connecting Calendar, enabling notifications)

These are sequenced last not because they're unimportant but because the UI is only
as good as the data and intelligence underneath it.

title: Calendar view modes: day, work-week, month
description:
Calendar section currently shows today + tomorrow only. As a planning tool it needs broader time horizons — especially work-week view for sprint planning and month view for deadline awareness. Only worth building once Google Calendar real data is in place.

Implementation Plan:
1. Add calView state: 'day' | 'workweek' | 'month' to CalendarSection. Add toggle button group to the section header.
2. Day view: current behavior — today + tomorrow, events as a time-sorted list.
3. Work-week view: Mon–Fri of the current week, grouped by day header. Within each day, events as a time-sorted list. No pixel-positioned grid needed.
4. Month view: 5-row × 7-column grid. Each cell shows day number + up to 3 event dots (colored by type). Overflow shown as '+N more'.
5. Clicking a day cell in month view expands that day's events in an inline popover below the grid.
6. All views consume the same normalised {id, title, start, end, date, type} shape from useGoogleCalendar — no view-specific fetching.
7. Depends on: life-5eu (Google Calendar OAuth — mock data is insufficient for multi-day views).
design: Add calView state: 'day' | 'workweek' | 'month'. Toggle buttons in CalendarSection header. Day: current behaviour (today + tomorrow, time-sorted event list). Work week: Mon-Fri of current week, grouped by day header, events as time-sorted list per day — no pixel-positioned grid needed. Month: 5-row × 7-col grid, each cell shows day number + up to 3 event dots (colored by type), overflow shown as '+N more'. Click a day cell in month view to expand that day's events in a popover. All views use the same normalised event shape from useGoogleCalendar hook. Depends on: Google Calendar OAuth integration.
notes: Part of: EPIC-POLISH (life-u86). Blocked by life-5eu (Calendar Context Collector) — cannot test multi-day views without real calendar data.

title: Netlify scheduled function for urgent task email notifications
description:
Server-side hourly check for tasks with deadline approaching, sends email digest. Requires Supabase (tasks must be server-accessible — localStorage is client-only). Prerequisite: Supabase integration must be complete.

Implementation Plan:
1. Install dependencies in netlify/functions/: npm install @supabase/supabase-js resend.
2. Create netlify/functions/notify-urgent.mts.
3. In netlify.toml add: [functions.notify-urgent] schedule = "0 * * * *" (runs hourly).
4. Function logic: init Supabase with service key (bypasses RLS). Query tasks WHERE deadline < NOW() + INTERVAL '24 hours' AND status = 'pending', joined to auth.users for email address.
5. Check notifications_sent table (task_id, sent_at) — skip tasks already notified. Send remaining via Resend API (resend.com — free tier 3000/month): subject 'Due tomorrow: <title>', plain text body with title, deadline, and bd close hint.
6. Insert sent records into notifications_sent to prevent duplicate emails on the next run.
7. Required Netlify env vars: RESEND_API_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, SUPABASE_URL.
8. Depends on: life-bok (Supabase integration must be live first).
design: 1. npm install @supabase/supabase-js resend (in netlify/functions/). 2. Create netlify/functions/notify-urgent.mts. In netlify.toml add: [functions.notify-urgent]\nschedule = '0 * * * *'. 3. Function logic: init Supabase with service key (bypasses RLS). Query: SELECT tasks.*, auth.users.email FROM tasks JOIN auth.users ON tasks.user_id=auth.users.id WHERE deadline < NOW()+INTERVAL '24 hours' AND status='pending'. 4. For each result, send via Resend API (resend.com — free tier 3000/month, simple REST API): subject 'Due tomorrow: <title>', plain text body with title + deadline + bd close hint. 5. Track sent notifications in a notifications_sent table (task_id, sent_at) to avoid duplicate emails. 6. Env vars needed in Netlify dashboard: RESEND_API_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, SUPABASE_URL.
close_reason: Superseded by EPIC-NOTIFY. Email dispatch now flows through the Rules Engine → Notification Dispatcher pattern rather than a standalone Netlify cron. Replaced by Email Dispatch sub-issue under the Notification & Dispatch epic.

title: Supabase integration for cross-device task storage
description:
Manual tasks live in localStorage only — no cross-device sync, no server-side access for scheduled notifications, and lost if browser data is cleared. Supabase provides PostgreSQL with Row Level Security, a JS client, and magic-link auth — unlocking cross-device sync and unblocking server-side email notifications.

Implementation Plan:
1. Create Supabase project at supabase.com. Run schema: CREATE TABLE tasks (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, user_id UUID REFERENCES auth.users, title TEXT, category TEXT, priority TEXT, time_required INT, deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT, source TEXT DEFAULT 'manual', source_url TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); ALTER TABLE tasks ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; CREATE POLICY 'own tasks' ON tasks USING (auth.uid()=user_id) WITH CHECK (auth.uid()=user_id).
2. npm install @supabase/supabase-js.
3. Add VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY to .env.local (gitignored) and to Netlify env vars.
4. Create useSupabaseTasks hook that mirrors the current useState+localStorage pattern but reads/writes to Supabase.
5. Auth: supabase.auth.signInWithOtp({email}) magic link. Minimal UI: email input + 'Send link' button, shown when not authenticated.
6. Migration path: on first Supabase login, if localStorage has tasks, offer a one-click import button.
7. Graceful degradation: if not logged in, fall back to localStorage (existing behavior unchanged).
design: 1. Create Supabase project at supabase.com. Run schema: CREATE TABLE tasks (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, user_id UUID REFERENCES auth.users, title TEXT, category TEXT, priority TEXT, time_required INT, deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT, source TEXT DEFAULT 'manual', source_url TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); ALTER TABLE tasks ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; CREATE POLICY 'own tasks' ON tasks USING (auth.uid()=user_id) WITH CHECK (auth.uid()=user_id). 2. npm install @supabase/supabase-js. 3. Add VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY to .env.local and Netlify env vars. 4. Create useSupabaseTasks hook that mirrors current useState+localStorage pattern but reads/writes Supabase. 5. Auth: supabase.auth.signInWithOtp({email}) magic link — minimal UI (email input + 'Send link' button). 6. Migration path: on first Supabase login, if localStorage has tasks, offer one-click import. 7. Graceful degradation: if not logged in, fall back to localStorage (existing behaviour).
close_reason: Superseded by EPIC-INFRA. Supabase is now the World State store (broader scope than tasks-only sync). Replaced by dedicated World State schema issue under the Infrastructure Foundation epic.

title: Design and generate real PWA icons
description:
Current icons are placeholder blue circles generated with Python. PWA install prompt and home screen show these — embarrassing for a tool you actually use daily. Need real branded icons before sharing or using the app seriously.

Implementation Plan:
1. Design the icon: 'LO' monogram or a calendar/organizer glyph on blue-600 (#2563eb) background. Must be readable at 48px and sharp at 512px. Create SVG as the canonical source file.
2. Export PNG assets: 192×192 → public/icon-192.png, 512×512 → public/icon-512.png, 180×180 → public/apple-touch-icon.png. Tools: Figma (free), Canva, or hand-coded SVG + Inkscape/sharp for PNG export.
3. Add apple-touch-icon to index.html <head>: <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">.
4. Add theme-color meta if not present: <meta name="theme-color" content="#2563eb">.
5. Verify vite-plugin-pwa config / public/manifest.json references the correct icon paths and sizes.
6. Test PWA install prompt on iOS Safari and Android Chrome — confirm home screen icon renders correctly at both sizes.
design: Design: simple 'LO' monogram or calendar/organizer glyph on blue-600 (#2563eb) background. Keep it readable at 48px (home screen) and sharp at 512px. SVG source is the canonical format. Exports needed: 192x192 PNG (public/icon-192.png), 512x512 PNG (public/icon-512.png), 180x180 PNG (public/apple-touch-icon.png — add to public/ and link in index.html <head>). Tools: Figma (free), Canva, or hand-coded SVG. For apple-touch-icon add: <link rel='apple-touch-icon' href='/apple-touch-icon.png'> to index.html. Also add <meta name='theme-color' content='#2563eb'> if not already present.
notes: Part of: EPIC-POLISH (life-u86). This is a standalone design task that can proceed independently of the infrastructure and intelligence epics.
